title: Robyn Is Still Dancing On Her Own
content:
Once upon the 1990s, a teenage girl named Robin Miriam Carlsson was crowned a pop princess. Her crystalline voice and secretive smile caught the attention of the Swedish record industry. Producers and songwriters quickly helped her create the swooning global hits "Show Me Love" and "Do You Know (What It Takes)." But Carlsson, first discovered at age 13, soon realized she didn't want to be a singing automaton or a mere vessel for the pop machine. She turned down a lucrative deal from the U.S. branch of Jive Records, which then set out to find an American version of her—and landed on Britney Spears.

By then, Robin Carlsson had reinvented herself as Robyn. A few years later, in 2005, she founded her own independent label to make her own kind of music. Her new sound combined firm dance beats, campy hip-hop flourishes, and synth riffs that spiraled and tessellated like the instruments in a Bach fugue. Her lyrics forcefully declared independence from clingy lovers and assorted societal expectations, often through analogies inspired by technology.

To simply quote her song titles from 2010's 'Body Talk,' a now-classic album, she was an "Indestructible" "Fembot" warning, "Don't Fucking Tell Me What to Do." Beneath the metallic veneer, though, her songs retained the tenderness and precision of a homily. The overall effect was to make solitude sound sexy, sad, and hopeful all at once. The timing had been perfect for her to liberate herself from industry constraints. The traditional music business was collapsing, as the internet cut into CD sales while letting listeners elevate their own niche idols.

Mainstream pop was going maximalist by overloading its production with digital whizbangery, while indie rock had risen as a rawer alternative. Robyn successfully split the difference. She expressed a rebellious worldview in a sleek and organized way, like a manifesto in a well-formatted digital document. That manifesto was exactly what 21st-century pop culture wanted and needed to hear. Spears had become a cautionary tale of a girl who gave her youth to the record industry and ended up losing her legal sovereignty.

Robyn did not become nearly as famous, but her emo bangers pointed the way for the likes of Lorde, Ariana Grande, and Taylor Swift. Poptimism, the ascendant belief that a genre ruled by formulas and artifice can contain plenty of originality and humanity, made Robyn its mascot. With time, her confident outlook on music came to seem like an insight into life itself. For many Millennials navigating their own independence, her journey served as an inspiring blueprint. Ultimately, Robyn proved that artistic freedom could yield enduring pop masterpieces.
